title_en: Zakat and Fasting in Al-Bahr ar-Ra'iq
content_en:
Ibn Nujaym's zakat and fasting chapters in Al-Bahr ar-Ra'iq provide thorough Hanafi treatment of these pillars, with characteristic attention to the school's distinctive positions and their evidential basis. Ibn Nujaym consistently identifies the position adopted for fatwa (the operative ruling for practice) while presenting the range of scholarly opinions within the school.

For zakat on gold and silver, Ibn Nujaym presents the Hanafi school's permission to pay zakat in monetary value (qimah) rather than physical commodity with its reasoning: zakat is fundamentally a mechanism for redistributing wealth to the poor, and money serves the poor's needs as well as (or often better than) physical commodities. The hadith 'Take from their wealth a charity by which you purify and sanctify them' (9:103) uses the word 'sadaqah' broadly, which the Hanafi school interprets as not restricting the form of payment to the specific commodity.

The Hanafi school's treatment of trade goods (urdu't-tijarah) in Al-Bahr ar-Ra'iq addresses the question of when goods held for re-sale cross from trade inventory (zakatable at end of hawl) to personal items (not zakatable). Ibn Nujaym presents the Hanafi criterion as intention: goods purchased with the specific intent to re-sell are trade goods and zakatable; goods initially purchased for personal use but later decided to be sold do not become trade goods until actively offered for sale.

For zakat al-fitr, Ibn Nujaym presents the Hanafi position that it may be paid early in Ramadan and that cash equivalents are valid. He also addresses a distinctive Hanafi position: the obligation arises at the moment of Fajr on 'Eid day (not at sunset the night before as other schools hold), meaning that a child born before Fajr on 'Eid day has zakat al-fitr due on their behalf, while one born after Fajr does not.

For Ramadan fasting, Ibn Nujaym's chapter is notable for its detailed treatment of medical interventions and their effect on the fast — a question that was becoming increasingly relevant in the Ottoman period with developments in medical practice. He applies the Hanafi principle that anything reaching the stomach or brain cavity through a natural opening breaks the fast, presenting the implications for various medical procedures including nosedrops, eardrops, enemas, and cauterization.

The chapter on i'tikaf presents the Hanafi school's position that i'tikaf may be performed in any mosque — not restricted to the three Sacred Mosques or to mosques with congregational prayer (jama'ah). However, the Hanafi school distinguishes between a sunnah i'tikaf (requiring fasting alongside seclusion, based on certain transmitted opinions) and a nafl i'tikaf (which may be performed without fasting). Ibn Nujaym discusses this distinction carefully, noting the evidential debates and the operative fatwa positions.
title_id: Zakat dan Puasa dalam Al-Bahr ar-Ra'iq
content_id:
Bab-bab zakat dan puasa karya Ibn Nujaym dalam Al-Bahr ar-Ra'iq menyajikan pembahasan Hanafi yang menyeluruh tentang dua rukun ini, dengan perhatian khas pada posisi-posisi khusus mazhab beserta landasan dalilnya. Ibn Nujaym secara konsisten mengidentifikasi posisi yang diadopsi untuk fatwa (hukum yang berlaku dalam praktik) sembari memaparkan ragam pendapat ulama dalam mazhab.

Untuk zakat emas dan perak, Ibn Nujaym memaparkan izin mazhab Hanafi membayar zakat dalam nilai mata uang (qimah) alih-alih komoditas fisik beserta penalarannya: zakat pada dasarnya merupakan mekanisme redistribusi kekayaan kepada fakir miskin, dan uang melayani kebutuhan fakir miskin sama baiknya (atau bahkan lebih baik) daripada komoditas fisik. Hadis "Ambillah dari harta mereka sedekah yang dengannya kamu menyucikan dan membersihkan mereka" (9:103) menggunakan kata "shadaqah" secara luas, yang ditafsirkan mazhab Hanafi sebagai tidak membatasi bentuk pembayaran pada komoditas tertentu.

Pembahasan mazhab Hanafi tentang barang dagangan ('urdu't-tijarah) dalam Al-Bahr ar-Ra'iq mengkaji kapan barang yang disimpan untuk dijual kembali bergeser dari inventaris dagang (terkena zakat di akhir hawl) ke barang pribadi (tidak terkena zakat). Ibn Nujaym menyajikan kriteria Hanafi sebagai niat: barang yang dibeli dengan niat khusus untuk dijual kembali adalah barang dagangan dan terkena zakat; barang yang awalnya dibeli untuk penggunaan pribadi tetapi kemudian diputuskan untuk dijual tidak menjadi barang dagangan sampai benar-benar ditawarkan untuk dijual.

Untuk zakat fitrah, Ibn Nujaym menyajikan posisi Hanafi bahwa boleh dibayar lebih awal di bulan Ramadan dan bahwa setara uang tunai adalah sah. Beliau juga membahas posisi Hanafi yang khas: kewajiban timbul pada saat Subuh hari Raya Idul Fitri (bukan saat terbenam matahari malam sebelumnya sebagaimana dipegang mazhab lain), yang berarti anak yang lahir sebelum Subuh pada hari Raya memiliki zakat fitrah yang wajib dibayarkan atas namanya, sementara yang lahir setelah Subuh tidak.

Untuk puasa Ramadan, bab Ibn Nujaym patut dicatat karena pembahasan rinci tentang intervensi medis dan pengaruhnya terhadap puasa — suatu persoalan yang semakin relevan pada masa Utsmaniyah seiring perkembangan praktik kedokteran. Beliau menerapkan prinsip Hanafi bahwa segala sesuatu yang mencapai rongga perut atau otak melalui lubang alami membatalkan puasa, memaparkan implikasinya untuk berbagai prosedur medis termasuk tetes hidung, tetes telinga, enema, dan kauterisasi.

Bab tentang i'tikaf memaparkan posisi mazhab Hanafi bahwa i'tikaf boleh dilaksanakan di masjid mana pun — tidak terbatas pada tiga Masjid Haram atau masjid yang memiliki salat berjamaah. Namun, mazhab Hanafi membedakan antara i'tikaf sunnah (yang mensyaratkan puasa beriringan dengan berdiam di masjid, berdasarkan pendapat-pendapat yang ditransmisikan) dan i'tikaf nafl (yang boleh dilaksanakan tanpa puasa). Ibn Nujaym membahas perbedaan ini secara cermat, mencatat perdebatan dalil dan posisi fatwa yang berlaku.
content_ar: Ibn Nujaym's الزكاة and الصيام الأبواب in Al-Bahr ar-Ra'iq provide thorough الحنفي treatment of these pillars, with characteristic attention to the school's distinctive positions and their evidential basis. Ibn Nujaym consistently identifies the position adopted for الفتوى (the operative ruling for practice) بينما presenting the range of العلمي opinions within the school. For الزكاة on gold and silver, Ibn Nujaym presents the الحنفي school's permission to pay الزكاة in monetary value (qimah) rather than physical commodity with its reasoning: الزكاة is بشكل أساسي a mechanism for redistributing الثروة to the poor, and المال serves the poor's needs as well as (or often better than) physical commodities. The الحديث 'Take from their الثروة a الصدقة by which you purify and sanctify them' (9:103) uses the word 'sadaqah' broadly, which the الحنفي school interprets as not restricting the form of payment to the specific commodity. The الحنفي school's treatment of trade goods (urdu't-tijarah) in Al-Bahr ar-Ra'iq addresses the question of عندما goods held for re-sale cross from trade inventory (zakatable at end of hawl) to personal items (not zakatable). Ibn Nujaym presents the الحنفي criterion as intention: goods purchased with the specific intent to re-sell are trade goods and zakatable; goods initially purchased for personal use but later decided to be sold do not become trade goods حتى actively offered for sale. For الزكاة al-fitr, Ibn Nujaym presents the الحنفي position that it may be paid early in Ramadan and that cash equivalents are valid. He أيضاً addresses a distinctive الحنفي position: the الالتزام arises at the moment of Fajr on 'Eid day (not at sunset the night قبل as other schools hold), meaning that a الولد born قبل Fajr on 'Eid day has الزكاة al-fitr due on their behalf, بينما one born بعد Fajr does not. For Ramadan الصيام, Ibn Nujaym's الباب is notable for its detailed treatment of medical interventions and their effect on the fast — a question that was becoming increasingly relevant in the Ottoman period with developments in medical practice. He applies the الحنفي principle that anything reaching the stomach or brain cavity through a natural opening breaks the fast, presenting the implications for various medical procedures بما في ذلك nosedrops, eardrops, enemas, and cauterization. The الباب on i'tikaf presents the الحنفي school's position that i'tikaf may be performed in any المسجد — not restricted to the three Sacred Mosques or to mosques with congregational الصلاة (jama'ah). غير أن, the الحنفي school distinguishes between a السنة i'tikaf (requiring الصيام alongside seclusion, based on certain transmitted opinions) and a النفل i'tikaf (which may be performed without الصيام). Ibn Nujaym discusses this distinction carefully, noting the evidential debates and the operative الفتوى positions.
